pageDescription: The insider scoop on some of my projects.
My PHP engine I'm building to run on the .NET CLR
keywords: PHPIL, PHP in C#, PHP on CLR, John Hudson

pageDescription: My favourite project so far. An open source CMS designed for static github pages websites.
A much faster implementation of PHPs Reflection library.
Lightweight PHP Canvas system for SSR and SPA pages with CSP-compliant page state and flexible rendering.
A custom language extension for C#, which allows JSX like syntax to be used in C#, allowing writing HTML in the C#.
My contributions to socialstack core along my employment at 4-roads.
A huge turning point in socialstack's frontend development.
A huge turning point in my career.
Find out the reason why I ended up building this.
content: [{"id":"e7f1561b-f9ad-4dc0-a90a-118f77a6a75e","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Before generating TypeScript entities and APIs, JavaScript IntelliSense was almost useless for our frontend developers. Our codebase used a custom Node resolver because pages were rendered as React canvases with a JSON tree describing components. While this setup was necessary for rendering, it caused the language server to run into repeated misdirection, making autocomplete unreliable or nonexistent. Developers could only get hints in limited cases, such as when JSDoc comments were applied to local variables. This workaround was brittle and not scalable for a project of our size.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"9f18f065-649b-4f1f-a5db-6931e1a918b2","component":"Text","data":{"content":"The lack of reliable type information created real friction. Developers spent significant time guessing property names, looking up API endpoints, and verifying payload structures manually. Refactoring was error-prone because there were no static checks for changes between backend data structures and frontend usage. Convincing the team to adopt TypeScript initially met resistance, as it seemed like extra overhead for existing JavaScript workflows.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"622e8a6a-7783-4cc9-8dc0-a5e09b4a380e","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Once the team saw how generating typed entities and APIs restored full code completion, static type checking, and autocomplete across the project, the shift became a major productivity win. Tasks that previously required tedious Ctrl+F lookups and trial-and-error coding could now be accomplished confidently with instant IDE hints. What was once a hesitant team quickly embraced the workflow, and the TypeScript-generated API approach became a favorite across the team.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]}]

pageDescription: Find out the reason why I ended up building this.
content: [{"id":"d04f6c88-8a24-4c5e-8944-8988487722c7","component":"Text","data":{"content":"The goal for the entities was to mirror the same type structure on the frontend as the backend, this would mean code completion becomes a thing in the IDE again, which was a huge productivity bonus, it went from Ctrl+F and symbol lookups while combing through files, to typescript dropping hints about objects. The development team immediately noticed a huge improvement to the developer experience, what was once a hesitant team, decided it was time to make the jump. ","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"d6bc815c-bf91-48f8-9ac5-6aa4f39858d2","component":"Text","data":{"content":"The next thing to do was collect all fields and properties, in order to follow convention with the types, I had to filter them by modifier flags, BindingFlags.Public | BindingFlags.Instance | BindingFlags.DeclaredOnly, this was to make sure that only fields declared by the entity class, were public, and weren't static. The reason we wanted public only, private fields won't be serialized by JSON serializers, so I skipped anything that wasn't public,I wasn't interested in static members, they're not going to be serialized either, and I used DeclaredOnly to make sure no fields from Content<ID> spilled into the descendant definitions, they should inherit them fields from the generated type for it's parent class. ","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"3670fe80-6a8e-4456-b263-4cc66a90613d","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Then I wrote the loop iterating the fields and the properties,  they're 2 seperate loops due to GetFields() returning FieldInfo[] whereas GetProperties() returns PropertyInfo[]. Inside that loop, I checked any cases where we could skip a field being generated, this I checked for attributes such as JsonIgnore. After all the checks occured, I had a list of usable fields/ properties I could write to the entity, the next step I had, was converting C# names to TypeScript. All the common number types were cast to number, [typeof(String)] = \"string\", [typeof(Boolean)] = \"boolean\", etc...  From there it was just a case of matching a type to either a preset type, or setting it to a generated type, and importing the reference. ","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"07ca85ec-bb4a-4ca5-b2f6-57432414e69f","component":"Text","data":{"content":"There was 1 TypeScript file generated per entity (not per type, per entity), I had also created a check, if the type was another content type, it imported that entity, otherwise it generated its own copy of the structure in the same file, every type was resolved fully. The field names where then camelcased, as the JSON response entity was camelcased, with matches JavaScript/TypeScript convention.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"83a55bce-9bc4-4d12-bc33-abfc4e24751e","component":"Text","data":{"content":"The last remaining tasks were to get all the virtual fields to generate too, I made these generate as potentially undefined, as in most cases they were, this was useful when typescript then threw up errors if something wasn't undefined/null checked, which meant so many less bugs caused by null objects etc... Virtual fields are linked entities, these are marked at entity level using an attribute called HasVirtualField(\"User\", typeof(User), \"UserId\"), this allowed includes to be a thing, and you can pull in related entities, just by adding the include on the endpoint, I'll talk more about that on the Controller page. ","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"b03a9e55-9983-4be6-8925-845f0215bb46","component":"Text","data":{"content":"In order to keep memory usage low, and keep generation fast, I used a StringBuilder, the reason I used a StringBuilder was due to string appends create intermediate strings, I also avoided LINQ, LINQ is nice syntax to work with, but can introduce iterator allocations, delegate calls, hidden enumerator structs and even creates additional allocations during some operations, the loops were kept simple, effective and highest performance bounty first, Keeping branching simple allows CPU branch prediction to be more effective, reducing unnecessary jumps and wasted cycles. It also keeps the code easier to reason about, reducing cognitive load for developers maintaining the generator.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]}]

pageDescription: Find out the reason why I ended up building this.
content: [{"id":"49ef48fb-323e-4d5b-9886-f728a55f4b5b","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Once entity generation was complete, the next step was generating the TypeScript APIs for the controllers. Each controller produced a corresponding TypeScript API class, typically placed alongside the generated entity types for that module. Some controllers did not have associated entities; in those cases an API class was still generated, but without the standard entity operations. For entity-backed controllers, a base AutoController API class was generated first, and entity-specific APIs inherited from it,  a base AutoController API was generated first, and the entity APIs inherited from it. Since TypeScript supports generics in a similar way to C#, it was straightforward to mirror the backend type signatures. This allowed common operations such as list, load, create, update, delete, and listAll to be implemented once in the base API and reused across all generated entity APIs.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"90b32ba8-06a6-4798-8c7b-d5842338d85a","component":"Text","data":{"content":"The generated APIs quickly became one of the most impactful improvements to the codebase. Previously, endpoints were called through a generic webRequest helper that returned loosely typed results. With the generated APIs, calling an endpoint returned a strongly typed Promise matching the backend return type. Each entity module exported a default instantiated API instance. Because ES modules are evaluated once and cached by the module loader, the API instance is created a single time and shared across all imports. This meant the API surface behaved effectively as a singleton while remaining easy to import and use throughout the application.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"98304453-f2f8-4473-a3fa-45161dc31787","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Controller methods were generated by inspecting the backend controllers via reflection. Each method was examined to determine its HTTP verb by looking for ASP.NET attributes such as HttpGetAttribute, HttpPostAttribute, HttpPutAttribute, HttpPatchAttribute, and HttpDeleteAttribute. Route templates defined within these attributes were parsed to detect URL parameters. When parameters were discovered in the route template (for example {id}), they were automatically added to the generated TypeScript method signature. Method parameters were also inspected to determine whether they represented route parameters, query parameters, or request payloads. Primitive values typically mapped to route or query parameters, while complex types were treated as request bodies. Return types were mapped using the same type conversion logic used for entities, translating Task<T> or ValueTask<T> into Promise<T> on the TypeScript side.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"d7e98cea-746a-4503-b9b3-3d13707d4530","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Another important piece was generating support for includes. On the backend, related entities could be requested by passing a comma-delimited list of virtual field names to the endpoint. While functional, this approach relied on manually constructing string values, which made it prone to typos and difficult to discover. To improve this, the generator produced a dedicated includes helper object for each API. Instead of writing arbitrary strings, developers could reference includes through a strongly typed structure exposed via getters. This allowed include chains to be built with full code completion support. For example, rather than writing a string like \"creatorUser,business.parentBusiness\", developers could instead reference userApi.includes.creatorUser or build deeper chains such as userApi.includes.business.parentBusiness.userId. This removed guesswork entirely and made the relationship graph between entities much easier to navigate from the client side.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"69dc64a4-2253-4fe9-a0cc-41ea3f1ba0f3","component":"Text","data":{"content":"To further simplify API consumption on the frontend, we also introduced a small useApi React hook. Prior to this, calling APIs typically required a combination of useState and useEffect, which created a lot of repetitive boilerplate and made simple data loading patterns more verbose than necessary. The useApi hook abstracted this pattern into a single reusable primitive. Instead of manually managing loading state, effect triggers, and cleanup logic, the hook accepts a closure returning a Promise, along with a dependency array that determines when the request should be re-executed.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"51d6ecc2-532b-4b0d-a7ef-a73a584c0e9d","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Internally, the hook behaves similarly to a useEffect that executes an asynchronous function whenever its dependencies change, but it also manages the associated state lifecycle automatically. The promise returned by the closure is resolved and its value is stored internally using useState, with the hook returning the resolved value to the caller. Because the request logic is provided as a closure, it allows the hook to defer execution until the effect runs, while still capturing any variables referenced in the dependency array.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"83d7ca53-3c7e-4eba-afde-3a244e3d1062","component":"Text","data":{"content":"This allowed API calls to be expressed in a very concise way. For example:","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"df5959dd-bd45-482a-88df-de8802f5b395","component":"CodeBlock","data":{"code":"const [user] = useApi(() => userApi.load(userId), [userId]);","language":"tsx","filename":"example side effect:","showCopy":"true","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"51651f37-5d8e-4303-b30a-36e575162041","component":"Divider","data":{"variant":"spacer","size":"md","label":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"2256b273-6a52-45d0-9dbf-15d4254d06bb","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Instead of manually writing several lines of state management and effect logic, a single line expresses both the dependency and the request. The hook automatically re-executes the request when the observed dependencies change, keeping the data in sync with the component state. Combined with the generated typed API clients, this made consuming backend endpoints extremely straightforward while still preserving full type safety.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"3c165c19-00dc-49cf-ac5a-3dad6556507e","component":"Text","data":{"content":"In practice, this significantly reduced boilerplate across the frontend codebase. Most components that needed to fetch data could express their requirements declaratively with a single hook call, while the underlying complexity of state management, effect execution, and promise resolution remained encapsulated inside the hook.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]},{"id":"7f26877c-ca26-4675-bfd4-790bb7381a1e","component":"Text","data":{"content":"Together, the generated controllers and entities created a strongly typed contract between the backend and frontend. Endpoint calls were no longer loosely typed requests but well-defined API methods that mirrored the server implementation. This significantly improved discoverability, reduced runtime errors caused by incorrect payloads or response assumptions, and made refactoring much safer as both sides of the contract evolved together.","tag":"p","align":"left","weight":"regular","color":"","fontSize":"","fontFamily":"Inter, sans-serif","lineHeight":"","letterSpacing":"","marginBottom":"","className":""},"children":[]}]
